EI When friend of the court becomes aware that the payer's condition meets the definition of incapacitation as defined in the current or subsequent Michigan Child Support Formula, monthly support charges shall abate and be temporarily reduced to zero effective the date that the friend of the court office provides notice of the abatement to the parties and to the court.
Support charges shall be reinstated effecUve 60 days after the incapacitation ends.

The motlier has been granted a Personal Protection Order (23-005907-PP) against the father.
The mother alfeges that the father has a past history of drug use and expressed concern that his alleged erratic behavior is related to current use.
WATSON, Defendant Defendant requested, and the Court signed an ex parte order suspending the Plaintiff's parenting time.

The Court has serious concerns regarding the state of the Plaintiff's mental health, which could be a culmination of the job loss, homelessness and family loss he has endured over the past several months.
The Court considered supervised parenting time, and the Plaintiff insisted that he would not exercise supervised parenting time.
IT IS HEREBY ORDERED that the Plaintiff, Andrew Pigors, shall obtain a mental health assessment and provide it to the Court.

o A May 16, 2025 order imposed a $250.00 filing bond in the custody case, that has functioned as a financial gate against my ability to bring forth necessary motions, especially after the same PPO/custody proceedings contributed to my job loss and housing instability and loss of my parenting time with my son.

o On October 29, 2025, I orally motioned to Disqualify Judge McNeill live during the 3id continued custody hearing in relation to the ex-parte suspension of my parenting time, detailing the pattern of one-sided rulings, remarks, and irregular handling of evidence.
She refused to recuse and continues to preside over both the PPO and custody matters.

Taken together, this pattern erodes public confidence, treats me not only differently from the opposing party but criminally and disrespectfully, and-in my view-violates the canons requiring impartiality, avoidance of ex parte communications, and a fair opportunity to be heard, especially given that Ms.Watson is an employee of the Kent County Prosecutors office, Family Court Division as a caseworker.
DET AILED FACTUAL BACKGROUND AND TIMELINE The complete chronology is set out in Appendix A - Master Timeline of PPO, Custody, and Related Proceedings in Pigors v.
Watson, which consolidates events from the PPO docket, custody docket, police reports, and hearing dates.

Initial PPO obtained in 2023 based on extreme and unproven allegations.
o In 2023, Emily Watson obtained a PPO against me in Case No.
o Emergency responders, when called, did not find injuries, ongoing danger, or evidence supporting criminal prosecution.
corroborated danger, the PPO became the foundation for later contempt proceedings and was later used to influence custody outcomes.
Service and family involvement in building the PPO case.

o Service and preparation of the PPO involved members of Emily's family, who also appear as actors in related housing and family-law events, further entangling family disputes with judicial proceedings.
PPO as leverage in custody and parenting-time disputes.

o Over time, the PPO became a tool to escalate benign or civil disputes into alleged "violations," culminating in a series of show-cause hearings and jail time, which in him caused job loss and housing instability that were later used against me in custody decisions.
Ex Parte Paretxting-Time Suspension and Lack of Notice (August 2025) 4.
August 6, 2025 - Ex parte order suspending all parenting time.
o On or about August 6, 2025, Judge McNeill signed an ex parte "emergency" order suspending all of my parenting time with my three- year-old son, Lincoln.
o Only after contacting the court and obtaining a copy of the order did I learn that my parenting time had been suspended.
o At an interim hearing on or about August 28, 2025, the court considered whether to continue or modify the ex parte suspension.
o After the August 28 hearing, Judge McNeill ordered me to obtain a psychological assessment from HealtliWest.
o The opposing party and the priblic could not obtain it as a normal court filing.
September 9, 2025 - Order continuing suspension, referencing off-record evaluation.

o On or about September 9, 2025, Judge McNeill issued a written order continuing the suspension of my parenting time "until further order," explicitly referencing tlie existence or content of the HealthWest evaluation.
o This order was entered without a full evidentiary hearing on the disputed allegations and without explicit findings that I posed a danger to Lincoln or that unsupervised parenting time was unsafe.

By mid-September 2025, as detailed in my Master Timeline (Appendix A), the ex parte suspension and its extensions had caused me to miss: Two regular alternating weekends in August (August 8-12 and August 22-26); The full Labor Day week block (August 29-September 2); * Additional alternating weekends in September (September 5 -9 and September 19-23).

On October 8, 2025, chambers finally sclieduled a "continuation" of the matter for October 29, 2025-nearly two months after the ex parte suspension and far beyond the 21-day hearing expectation reflected in discussions of MCR 3.207(B)(6) in Michigan appellate practice.
The evidentiary hearing on the merits of tlie allegations and the parenting-time suspension finally concluded on October 29, 2025-approximately ninety days after the ex parte order.
Only at this late stage did I receive a full opportunity for sworn testimony and cross-examination.
PPO Contempt Proceedirxgs and Their Collateral Harm 13.

At a prior PPO show-cause hearing (coini'nonly referred to as Show Cause #4), I was jailed for alleged PPO contempt under contested circumstances, without a full opportunity to present exculpatory evidence.

These job and housing losses were later used against me in custody and parenting- time analysis, even though they directly flowed from the court's handling of PPO contempt, not from parental unfitness.
February 28, 2025 - PPO violation hearing (Counts #5 and #6).

At the February 28, 2025 PPO violation hearing, the record reflects side-channel communication minimizing the immediate threat of jail, while in open court the judge extended the PPO term and lectured me harshly.

Given my financial situation (made worse by the court-ordered jail time and its consequences), this bond operated as a practical bar to seeking relief, cliilling my ability to challenge ongoing violations or seek modification.
As a self-represented litigant facing an experienced opposing party and intertwined PPO/custody issues, these actions effectively denied me a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
The Motion to Disqualify and its supporting exhibits are attached to this complaint as part of Appendix B.

o Directing that a critical mental-health evaluation be emailed to chambers instead of filed, and reviewing a USB recording through staff outside my presence, are forms of ex parte evidence handling inconsistent with Canon 3 and with the requirement that decisions be based on the record.

(FindLaw Case Law) o In my case, an ex parte order completely suspending parenting time remained in effect for approximately tl'iree months before a full evidentiary hearing occurred, despite my objection and repeated requests, and without detailed best-interest findings during the extended suspension.
Canon 3(A)(3) - Patience, dignity, and courtesy; fair opportunity to be heard.

o Muting my microphone, cutting off objections, and telling me not to file additional materials because they would not be considered, particularly wl'iere the other side had counsel, contradicts the duty to treat litigants with patience, dignity, and courtesy, and to ensure a fair opportunity to be heard.

It is about a systemic pattern over multiple years, across both PPO and custody cases, that appears to violate Canon 1, Canon 2, and Canon 3, and that has severely harmed my parental relationship and public confidence in the Muskegon family court.

Because of the ex parte suspension and the court's decisions to continue it without timely hearing: * My three-year-old son has been deprived of regular, loving contact with his father for months at a time.
Job loss and housing instability, directly traceable to PPO contempt proceedings and jail time, have themselves been used against me in later analysis, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of prejudice.
That is the opposite of what Canon 1 and Canon 2 envision.

The Court has serious concerns regarding the state of the Plaintiff's mental health, which could be a culmination of the job loss, homelessness and family loss he has endured over the past several months.
The Court considered supervised parenting time, and the Plaintiff insisted that he would not exercise supervised parenting time.
The Court then issued a written order stating the Father to obtain a mental health assessment and provide it to the Court.
* He has support of friends and family.

Analysis The Mother met her initial burden of a change in circumstances as required under Pierron, as the Father's actions in contacting her family, his erratic behavior and general mental health deterioration are a change in circumstances to review parenting time.
The Court finds that the following fadors are applicable: the mental and physical health of the parties involved: The Court has serious concerns * about the Father's mental health.

Domestic violence, regardless of whether the violence was directed against or * witnessed by the child: The Father has a PPO against him.
